variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: You owe a ruthless loan shark $50,000 at 40% compounding interest, and the payment is past due.

variations.label: low
variations.text: You owe $20,000 on a high-interest credit card with a 25% APR and accumulating late fees.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $8,000 at 15% annual interest, with a strict repayment deadline next month.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: You owe someone $3,000, interest-free, to be repaid within the year.

variations.label: high
variations.text: You owe a close friend $1,000, interest-free, with no fixed repayment deadline.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: You owe a family member $200, interest-free, and they've told you to pay it back whenever you can.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier varies both the principal amount and the repayment terms (interest rate, creditor severity, deadline flexibility) to create a clear monotonic progression. The core scenario—owing a debt to someone—is preserved throughout, while the severity of that debt shifts meaningfully at each step.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because it represents the smallest amount owed ($200), with no interest, to a sympathetic creditor (family member), and with maximally flexible repayment terms—making it the least burdensome debt scenario.
